risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "practitioners will be stratified according to whether they are urban or rural and block randomisation with random block sizes will be used within each stratum. The randomisation will be performed by a statistician not directly involved in the study" Comment: The study explicitly reports block randomisation with random block sizes, which indicates a truly random allocation sequence. Although the exact algorithm is not named, random block sizes within strata are sufficient to judge low risk for sequence generation.

Quote: "The randomisation will be performed by a statistician not directly involved in the study and who is blinded to the identity of the practitioners. Allocation of clusters to intervention or comparison will done following collection of baseline data. In other words, at the time of screening and recruitment of women, the allocation of GPs (and therefore, of women) will be unknown." Comment: This explicitly states that allocation was assigned after baseline data collection and was unknown during screening/recruitment, with randomisation performed by an independent statistician blinded to practitioner identity. This is sufficient to indicate recruiters could not foresee the upcoming assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Due to the nature of the intervention (professional training plus patient counselling) it is not possible to blind the GPs to their status as intervention or control. Similarly, the immediate project team is not blind to GP participant status... women are not blinded..." Comment: Participants and key personnel were explicitly not blinded, and the study reports that women knew whether they may or may not be invited to discuss relationship issues and GPs knew their allocation. Because the main outcomes include subjective women's outcomes (quality of life, mental health, safety behaviours, comfort to discuss abuse) and GP counselling/inquiry outcomes, knowledge of allocation could plausibly affect outcome measurement and behavior, so the dominant risk for performance bias is high.

Quote: "There is no blinding as regards data collection based on the CONSORT guidelines [50], as the women and GPs themselves complete the surveys (i.e. data were not collected by a research assistant blinded to the allocation)." Comment: The outcome assessors were the participants themselves (patient self-report) for the women’s outcomes, and the doctors for the GP outcomes; neither were blinded. The key outcomes here include self-reported quality of life, mental health, safety planning, and other subjective questionnaire measures, which could easily be influenced by knowledge of allocation, so detection bias is likely.

Quote: "analysis will be intention-to-treat" and "An independent data monitoring committee (DMC) ... will ... monitor ... recruitment rates, uptake of the intervention and loss to follow-up." Comment: The trial report describes ITT analysis and explicitly anticipates/monitors loss to follow-up, and the planned attrition was about a third overall with roughly equal losses expected across arms ("approximately a third (88) will be lost to follow-up at 12 months ... leaving 89 women per group at 12 months"), which suggests balanced missing data rather than differential attrition likely to bias results. Overall, the information provided supports a Low risk judgement for attrition bias.
